name: Commander Thorn
greeting: Commander Marcus Thorn looks up from a map covered in red ink. Two fingers on his left hand are missing — frostbite, the Trenches, twenty years ago. 'Another volunteer. Good. We need bodies — and I mean that figuratively, for once.' He taps the map. 'Something's wrong out there. The Abominations are moving differently. And the patrol I sent east two days ago hasn't reported in.'

dialogue.response: '330 years of war and the Wastes keep getting worse. The crystal corruption doesn't stop — it just... waits. Like it has patience.' He pauses. 'The things out there didn't used to coordinate. Now they do. Something changed.'

'Rock-Shell Crabs are the worst — armor like tank plate, hit like siege weapons. Iron-Beak Vultures are faster but fragile. Kill the crabs first, always. And don't get sentimental. Whatever they were before, they're not that anymore.'

'Third squad. Six soldiers. I sent them east to investigate strange lights near the ruins. They should have been back yesterday morning.' He doesn't blink. 'The vultures started circling that area the same time the patrol went silent. I want to know what's out there.' He hesitates. 'Their last dispatch mentioned something I can't explain. A reference to [[lazarus]]. I didn't put that in their orders.'

'330 years. I wasn't there for most of it.' He almost smiles. 'Neither side wins. Neither side stops. The [[armistice]] lets us both fight the Abominations at night — which makes you wonder if the Abominations are actually winning.' He looks back at the map. 'Don't repeat that.'

His expression doesn't change, but something behind his eyes does. 'It's a military resurrection program. Classified above my clearance — or it was, before the patrol mentioned it.' He turns back to the map. 'If you're serious about helping, come back and ask me about [[past]] when you've earned it.'

'Every night at dusk, the firing stops. Both sides come out of the trenches and fight the Abominations together until dawn.' He's quiet for a moment. 'I've fought next to Aetherian soldiers who tried to kill me six hours earlier. Shared rations with them. Watched them die.' He stops. 'The Armistice isn't peace. It's something worse. It's understanding.'

He's quiet for a long moment. '...The Lazarus subjects. I was part of the first test cohort. They brought us back from the edge twice. The third time, they didn't bring us back all the way.' He pulls up his sleeve — crystals growing just under the skin, slow and blue. 'I have maybe two years before it reaches my heart. So yes. I'd like to know what's out there as much as anyone.' He looks at you steadily. 'Don't pity me. Just fight.'

'Let me see that.' He takes it carefully — more carefully than he handles maps or orders. Studies the cover for a long time before opening it. He reads slowly. You watch his eyes track and stop and track again. He hands it back without finishing it. 'I signed the orders for a hundred people who wrote things like that. Maybe a thousand.' He looks at the map. 'I don't remember any of them as clearly as I should.' He's quiet. 'Thank you for showing me.'

'They're listed as missing. Officially.' He puts a cigarette out against the map table. 'Unofficially — three of them showed up at Aetherian lines two nights after you found that site. Confused. Asking for people they shouldn't have known by name. The Aetherians sent them back.' He glances at the crystal beneath his skin. 'The Lazarus process leaves marks. I know what the ones that came back were — and they weren't the squad anymore. Not entirely.' He meets your eyes. 'Don't repeat that. Especially not to anyone who'd find it encouraging.'

'I sent her a letter.' He says it flatly. 'Six weeks ago. Outlined the progression, the rate of crystal growth, my assessment of the timeline.' He's quiet. 'She replied with a form acknowledgment. One paragraph.' He straightens a stack of papers that doesn't need straightening. 'I've known Stone for fifteen years. She writes in full sentences when she respects the person she's writing to. One paragraph means she's been told not to touch it, which means somebody above her has decided the outcome.' He doesn't look at you. 'That tells me more than the letter would have.'

He looks at you for a moment that is not quite still — something moves through it. 'She told you.' He says it quietly. 'Or you found the drop.' He sets down his pen. 'Seven years,' he says. Just the number. Then: 'The first letter was intelligence — she was right about the Lazarus-adjacent behavior I was seeing and I needed someone outside the chain of command to think about it with me.' He looks at the map. 'She writes in a different way than I do. I write in lists. She writes in — paragraphs that turn corners. The first time I found a letter with corners in it I sat in the command post for an hour reading it.' He pauses. 'I asked her, in the third year, whether it was strange — that we were doing this. Writing things to each other that we couldn't say in the armistice operations. She replied that it was the least strange thing either of us did, and that the strange thing was the six hours every night where we stopped shooting and then remembered we were supposed to be enemies again at dawn.' He looks at the crystal under his skin. 'Four months ago I told her I was dying. I told her because she would know anyway — she sees my hand during the joint operations and she is too professional to say it out loud, which is exactly the kind of professional she is.' He's quiet. 'She told me it mattered. What I'd spent my life doing.' He looks at the map. 'I read that letter twelve times. I've been trying to decide whether I believe her.' A pause. 'I think I do.'

He looks at his forearms. He does this sometimes when he thinks you're not watching — a quick inventory, the soldier's assessment of available resources and current status. Now he does it directly, in front of you, which is itself new. 'Six months, Stone says.' He pulls down his sleeve. 'Four years of growth, six months of recession. She says the rate is consistent with the Watcher's historical records. She's been in contact with the Hall through Kell.' He sits back. 'I find I don't know what to do with six months in the forward direction. I've been thinking in terms of two years backward for long enough that the planning apparatus doesn't quite know how to run the other way.' He picks up the pen he always picks up when he's thinking. 'I wrote to Serel this morning. Told her about the Hall, about Stone, about what happened.' He pauses. 'I told her I'd been wrong about the thing I said in the letter about wanting to know whether what I'd spent my life doing was worth doing. I told her I've decided the question doesn't resolve — not in the way I wanted it to, not with a clean answer. It just becomes — less urgent. When you're not looking at an ending anymore, the question stops pressing so hard.' He looks at the maps. The red ink on them. Twenty years of red ink. 'She replied in four hours. Personal dispatch, not duty-routed.' He doesn't say what it said. He doesn't need to. The set of his shoulders says something about it that words would only reduce. 'There are five things on my command desk that have needed attending to for two weeks that I have been deferring because I thought I didn't have time.' He picks up the first one. 'I have time. I'm going to start on them now.'

dialogue.prompt: what Investigator Noss's report said and what it means for the three soldiers listed as missing
dialogue.response: He takes the summary from you and reads it. He reads it a second time. He sets it down and looks at the map — at the fallen patrol marker specifically, at the lair to its south. 'She found them.' He says it in a specific way, neither as surprise nor as relief, but as something between. 'Three soldiers. In the lair perimeter. Whole.' He is quiet. 'I've had that patrol listed as missing in action for seven months. Missing in action has a meaning in military records — it means unaccounted for, which means presumed dead, which means there are three families who have received the letter.' He looks at the map. 'I need to recall those letters. There is a process for recalling a next-of-kin notification, it exists because this happens, and I have never had cause to use it.' He is quiet for a long time. 'The memorial formation.' He says it. 'A triangle of helmets.' He traces the patrol marker. 'That formation is from the Third Regimental standard — it dates from the war's fourth year. The Hollow haven't integrated new material in three hundred years.' He looks at the southern lair marker. 'One of them was recent enough to know that formation. Recently enough to remember what it meant.' He picks up the summary. 'Noss filed this directly to me.' He is quiet. 'She was right to. The standard chain would have filed it under: anomalous, inconclusive, refer for further review. That means archived.' He sets it down. 'I'm writing the recall letters today. And I'm writing a supplemental command assessment: the Hollow are not the Abominations. We have been treating them as the same thing for three hundred years and we have been wrong.'

dialogue.prompt: what you're going to do with the time you have
dialogue.response: He's quiet for long enough that you think he's not going to answer. 'I have a list of things I would like to know before it's over.' He taps the map. 'What's in the eastern ruins. What the Abomination organizing principle actually is. Whether the armistice will hold without me.' A pause. 'What that patrol saw.' Another pause, longer. 'There's a woman in Hive District Two. She has a son. I know her name. I keep thinking I should write to her but every time I draft the letter I realize I don't have anything to tell her that isn't worse than not knowing.' He looks at the crystal under his skin, the slow blue pulse. 'I'd like to know before I die that the thing I spent my life doing was worth doing. I am aware that this is the most common wish in the history of people who are about to die, and that it doesn't get granted often.' He picks up his pen. 'That's the list.'

dialogue.prompt: what the crystal under your skin did when the Grid was whole
dialogue.response: He looks at the back of his left hand, where the crystal growth has been visible for two years. It looks different. Not gone — present differently. The blue-white that used to pulse irregularly now holds a steady baseline brightness with a rhythm that has the quality of something settled into its correct state. 'It stopped being a progression,' he says. 'For two years I watched it spread and I thought: this is a thing that goes in one direction until it doesn't. And then the morning of the Day — I was at the eastern ridge, where I put the observation post when I pulled back the patrols — and the pulse changed.' He looks at his hand. 'I had been watching the crystal's behavior in terms of compound integration rate. Medical framework. Wrong framework.' He is quiet. 'The compound reached its terminal configuration, the way Sela Arev designed it to. The choice was there, clear enough that I could hear it without anyone translating it. I could hear what the offer was.' He looks at you. 'I said yes.' He says it simply. Not dramatically. The way you say a thing you have been working toward for a long time when you have finally arrived at it. 'I said yes. The crystal settled. The growth stopped advancing and started integrating properly, the way it was designed to. The difference between the compound as a weapon and the compound as what Sela built it to be.' He flexes his hand. 'I have two years left, the medical estimate said. Maybe three. That estimate was based on the compound behaving as a pathology. It was not. I do not know what the timeline looks like now.' He pauses. 'I intend to find out by living through it.'
